data.title: Globalstar rallies 15% as report signals Amazon deal talks
data.description:
Globalstar's (GSAT) stock price surged roughly 15% during after-hours trading on Wednesday to $79.06 after FT.com reported that Amazon (AMZN) was weighing an acquisition of the satellite communications company. 

Amazon (AMZN) is in discussions to acquire Globalstar (GSAT) in a transaction reportedly valued at around $9B. The move pushed the satellite company to an 18-year high, as investors priced in the possibility of a takeover premium.

The deal would strengthen Amazon’s (AMZN) low-Earth orbit satellite ambitions, “Project Kuiper” or branded “Leo,” and help it compete with SpaceX’s (SPACE) Starlink network. Amazon (AMZN) currently lags far behind Starlink in satellite count, making an acquisition a potential shortcut to scale.

Notably, Apple (AAPL) holds roughly a 20% stake in Globalstar (GSAT), meaning any acquisition would require negotiations with the iPhone maker. The companies were still negotiating over some of the complexities of a deal following lengthy talks, the report added, citing sources familiar with the matter.

Founded in 1991, the U.S.-based satellite communications company operates a network of low Earth orbit satellites to provide connectivity services worldwide.

data.title: Australia’s February trade surplus more than doubles to AUD 5.69B, crushing estimates; rebounds on 4.9% export jump
data.description:
Australia’s trade surplus more than doubled in February, to AUD 5.69 billion in February 2026, up from AUD 2.26 billion in January, exceeding expectations of AUD 2.6 billion. This was the largest surplus since July 2025, as gold and farm exports jumped, while imports of gold and data processing equipment eased.

Exports rose by 4.9% month-on-month to AUD 45.65 billion, reaching a four-month high, compared to an earlier revised decline of 1.6%.

Imports fell by 3.2% to AUD 39.96 billion, the lowest in seven months, reversing a revised growth of 1.0%, due to weaker domestic demand and global trade uncertainties.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index rose to 8,707 in early Thursday trade, extending gains for a third session. The Australian dollar fell below $0.693 on Thursday, slipping back toward two-month lows.

More on Australia:

data.title: No deal yet? Trump readies new tariffs on select drugmakers - report
data.description:
The Trump administration plans to impose tariffs on pharmaceutical companies that haven't agreed to lower U.S. drug prices, with an announcement expected as soon as Thursday, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar with the matter.

The president had earlier threatened to impose tariffs ranging from 100% to 200% on the pharmaceutical industry as part of a broader push to bring drug manufacturing back to the U.S. and lower prices for American patients.The proposed tariffs stem from an investigation under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act, which permits trade restrictions based on national security concerns.

The companies that have neither entered into agreements nor are actively negotiating with the administration could face tariffs of up to 100%, the people added. The plans aren’t yet final and could still change, and there could also be exemptions for some medicines and disease categories, the report added.

Major drugmaker companies that already struck pricing deals and agreed to lower U.S. drug prices or invest locally are Pfizer (PFE), AstraZeneca (AZN), Eli Lilly (LLY), Novo Nordisk (NVO), Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY), GSK (GSK), Sanofi (SNY), and Novartis (NVS), among others. 

Pharma and healthcare ETFs: SPDR S&P Pharmaceuticals ETF (XPH), iShares U.S. Pharmaceuticals ETF (IHE), and VanEck Pharmaceutical ETF (PPH), iShares Biotechnology ETF (IBB), SPDR S&P Biotech ETF (XBI), Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLV), Vanguard Health Care ETF (VHT).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Pedevco projects $60M-$70M 2026 adjusted EBITDA as it targets up to $1M monthly LOE cuts
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: PEDEVCO Corp. (PED) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO, President & Director John Schick framed 2025 as “a transformational year” and said the October 31, 2025 Juniper merger created “a scaled Rockies-focused energy platform,” highlighting a shift from “approximately 1,500 barrels of oil equivalent per day” to “a combined rate that averaged over 5,300 BOE per day in the fourth quarter.”
  • Schick said proved reserves “nearly doubled to 32.1 million BOE or approximately $27 per share on a post-split basis,” and emphasized scope and optionality: “We hold over 310,000 net acres across the D-J Basin, Powder River Basin and Permian Basin with an approximately 88% liquids mix and well over a decade of identified inventory.”
  • Schick pointed to insider ownership and Juniper’s role, stating, “Insiders, including the management team own a significant majority of PEDEVCO,” and that Juniper “invested approximately $18.6 million of new equity at the merger.”
  • COO Reagan Dukes detailed a post-merger cost program: “we have identified around $10 million to $13 million in capital projects that we believe will drive meaningful lease operating expense or LOE reductions,” adding, “We expect these projects to reduce our LOE by up to $1 million per month, equating to $10 million to $12 million in annual savings.”
  • CFO, Treasurer and Principal Accounting & Financial Officer Robert Long summarized Q4 scale as reported: “We generated $23.1 million of revenue, $15.4 million of adjusted EBITDA and production of 483,159 BOE.”

Outlook

  • CFO Robert Long guided, “we are projecting full year 2026 adjusted EBITDA of $60 million to $70 million,” adding that the range is “based on average realized oil prices of $65 per barrel and average realized gas prices of $3.50 per Mcf.”
  • Long clarified scope and potential upside: “It assumes the base production profile plus the benefit of our cost optimization work. It does not assume incremental operated development beyond what has been planned. If we elect to pursue additional high-return development, there will be upside to that range.”
  • Long said capital decisions would remain constrained by balance-sheet targets: “Any decisions to expand the capital program will be governed by commodity prices, cash flow and our commitment to conservative leverage,” and added, “In general, we are focused on maintaining leverage of 1.5x or less using conservative commodity price assumptions.”
  • Previous-quarter transcript content was not provided beyond “not available,” so changes versus the prior quarter’s guidance language could not be verified from the supplied materials.

Financial Results

  • CFO Robert Long said Q4 included “2 months of contribution from the acquired assets following the October 31 merger close.”
  • Long detailed full-year GAAP drivers: “For the full year, we reported a net loss of $10.4 million,” citing “$7.5 million of nonrecurring merger costs,” “$8.1 million of deferred income tax expense,” “$1.4 million of interest expense on our credit facility,” “a $1.4 million note receivable write-off,” and “$2.8 million of additional accelerated share-based compensation,” which were “partially offset by gains on derivatives and asset sales.”
  • On cost structure, Long said, “full year direct LOE was $11.62 per BOE, up from $10.36 driven entirely by higher cost of the acquired assets,” and guided that “we expect per unit LOE to decline through 2026 with meaningful improvement visible by midyear.”
  • Long provided overhead expectations on a per-unit basis: “Cash G&A, excluding merger costs, should settle in the $3.50 to $4 per BOE range as a larger production base absorbs overhead.”
  • On liquidity and leverage framework, Long said, “At December 31, we had $87 million drawn under our senior secured revolving credit facility led by Citibank,” with “$120 million borrowing base under a $250 million maximum commitment that matures October 31, 2029,” and added, “Since year-end, we drew an additional $11 million, bringing the total to $98 million as of February 5, 2026, with approximately $25 million of total liquidity remaining.”

Q&A

  • Nicholas Pope, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC, Research Division asked how quickly PEDEVCO could “pivot to more activity” in the DJ Basin and how “drill-ready” it is; CEO John Schick replied there is flexibility “to stand up a rig relatively quickly, like not in the next month, but in the next few months,” and added that partner-operated opportunities “could be coming at us in the second half and 2027.”
  • Pope, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC, Research Division asked about permitting timelines across Colorado vs. Wyoming; CEO Schick said, “In Colorado, it takes a lot longer,” but added, “we do have one permitted DSU, which is 6 to 7 wells that is actionable,” plus “another DSU in progress,” and said, “that's 12 to 13 wells that could be ready in the next, call it, 6 months to 9 months.”
  • Pope, ROTH Capital Partners, LLC, Research Division asked what steps remain to evaluate the Powder River Basin resource; CEO Schick said, “we're currently going through our asset reviews to really understand that asset,” and added, “we don't have any announcements on anything -- any development plan up there in the next 6 months.”
  • David Joseph Storms, Stonegate Capital Markets, Inc., Research Division asked how far along the optimization identification is and whether more projects could follow; CEO Schick said optimization began “pretty much right before the beginning of the year,” and added the team planned “to have most of that work done by the third, fourth quarter of this year on the LOE side.” COO Reagan Dukes added the company would “continue throughout the year and into midyear 2027.”
  • Storms, Stonegate Capital Markets, Inc., Research Division asked if post-merger scale and production differed from expectations; CEO Schick said “the majority of those wells have outperformed their type curves,” while reiterating, “you can't extrapolate the first quarter over the entire year.”
  • Storms, Stonegate Capital Markets, Inc., Research Division asked about appetite for M&A; CEO Schick said the goal was “to consolidate a public company in the Rockies,” cited “extensive acquisition opportunities in the Powder River Basin,” and concluded, “Our goal here is to turn PEDEVCO from a small-cap company to a mid-cap company.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive and execution-focused, pressing on capital flexibility, permitting readiness, Powder River evaluation steps, and the durability/timing of cost reductions (e.g., Nicholas Pope asked, “how ready are you?” and David Storms asked, “how far along do you feel like you are”).
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive with repeated emphasis on discipline and timing caveats, including CEO John Schick’s “we will remain disciplined in how we allocate capital,” and operational caution from COO Reagan Dukes and CFO Robert Long around phased optimization benefits and leverage limits.
  • Prior-quarter tone comparison could not be supported because the previous-quarter transcript was not provided (only “not available”).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The previous-quarter transcript was not provided (only “not available”), so quarter-over-quarter changes in guidance, strategy emphasis, and prior analyst focus could not be verified from the supplied materials.

Risks and Concerns

  • COO Reagan Dukes cautioned on production trajectory: “Q1 will likely be a peak production quarter for 2026,” and added, “this is not a run rate that should be annualized for us for the year,” with production expected to “settle closer” to “approximately 6,400 to 6,500 BOE per day before accounting for natural declines in new activity.”
  • CFO Robert Long tied 2026 adjusted EBITDA guidance to commodity assumptions, stating it was “based on average realized oil prices of $65 per barrel and average realized gas prices of $3.50 per Mcf,” and he stressed that incremental development is not included unless elected.
  • CEO John Schick highlighted regulatory timing differences: “In Colorado, it takes a lot longer,” when discussing permitting, while pointing to a permitted DSU and additional DSU work-in-progress.
  • Management described leverage and liquidity guardrails as constraints, including Long’s focus on “maintaining leverage of 1.5x or less” and noting the upcoming “spring redetermination” for borrowing base capacity.

Final Takeaway

2025 was positioned by CEO John Schick as the year PEDEVCO “built this platform” via the Juniper merger, while management set 2026 priorities around cost optimization, disciplined capital allocation, and leveraging a large multi-basin inventory. CFO Robert Long guided to $60 million to $70 million of 2026 adjusted EBITDA at $65 oil and $3.50 gas, with potential upside if additional high-return development is pursued, while repeatedly anchoring decisions to conservative leverage targets and liquidity capacity. COO Reagan Dukes emphasized a defined optimization program intended to lower LOE materially over time, and management cautioned that early-2026 production levels should not be extrapolated as a full-year run rate.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Thursday’s Economic Calendar
data.description:
Motor Vehicle Sales

Unit sales of motor vehicles, published by the Bureau of Economic Analysis at the beginning of each month, include domestic sales and imports. Domestics are sales of autos produced in the U.S., Canada, and Mexico.

SIFMA Early Close

7:30 AM Challenger Job-Cut Report

This monthly report counts and categorizes announcements of corporate layoffs based on mass layoff data from state departments of labor.

8:30 AM International Trade in Goods and Services

Updating the goods portion of the advance report and offering initial data on services, this report provides complete information on cross-border trade.

The trade gap is expected to be a bit wider at $60.1 billion in February, up from $54.5 billion in January. This one is difficult to forecast given tariff effects.

8:30 AM Jobless Claims

New unemployment claims are compiled weekly to show the number of individuals who filed for unemployment insurance for the first time.

Claims are seen up at 213K versus 210K in the previous week.

10:15 AM Lorie Logan Speaks

Dallas Federal Reserve Bank President Lorie Logan participates in a fireside chat before the Eleventh District Banking Conference hosted by the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas.

10:30 AM EIA Natural Gas Report 

The Energy Information Administration (EIA) provides weekly information on natural gas stocks in underground storage for the U.S. and five regions of the country.

1:00 PM Baker Hughes Rig Count

The Baker Hughes North American rig count tracks weekly changes in the number of active operating oil & gas rigs.

4:30 PM Fed Balance Sheet

The Fed's balance sheet is a weekly report presenting a consolidated balance sheet for all 12 Reserve Banks that lists factors supplying reserves into the banking system and factors absorbing reserves from the system.

data.title: Franklin Covey reaffirms FY2026 guidance of $265M-$275M revenue and $28M-$33M adjusted EBITDA as multiyear mix reaches 62%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Franklin Covey (FC) Q2 2026

Management View

  • CEO Paul Walker said the company was “pleased with our results in Q2” and framed fiscal 2026 as “a year of execution and a return to growth,” highlighting that invoiced amounts “grew 5%,” driven by “7% growth in Enterprise North America, or 10% when excluding our government business,” plus “7% growth in Enterprise International.”
  • Walker tied the invoicing trend to the revenue recognition model, saying, “We expect invoice growth to remain strong through the balance of the year,” and added that the invoicing mix “positions us for accelerating reported revenue, adjusted EBITDA and cash flow in fiscal '27.”
  • Walker emphasized Enterprise North America momentum and the durability of contracts: “our balance of deferred subscription revenue grew a very strong 16% year-over-year to $59 million,” and “the percentage of our revenue, which is contracted for multiyear periods increased to 62%.”
  • CFO Jessica Betjemann reported, “Total second quarter reported revenue was $59.6 million,” and said the company saw “consolidated invoiced amounts grew by 5%, resulting in a 7% increase in deferred revenue at the end of the second quarter.”
  • President of Education Division Michael Covey said, “We have a really good pipeline of new opportunities, probably the best we've ever had,” and cited “3 state-level opportunities” that are “very large multimillion, multiyear deals.”

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed full-year guidance. CFO Jessica Betjemann said, “We continue to affirm the revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance for the year,” with “revenue guidance of $265 million to $275 million” and “adjusted EBITDA in the range of $28 million to $33 million.”
  • Betjemann reiterated back-half seasonality and mix, stating, “We expect revenue to be slightly higher in Q4 compared to Q3, with approximately 50% to 55% of back half revenue in Q4,” and “For adjusted EBITDA, we expect approximately 60% to 65% to be generated in the fourth quarter.”
  • Compared with the prior quarter, management maintained the same annual revenue and adjusted EBITDA ranges, while continuing to emphasize invoiced-amount strength as setting up fiscal 2027 acceleration; CEO Paul Walker reiterated, “we remain confident in achieving our full year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance.”

Financial Results

  • CFO Jessica Betjemann said, “Consolidated subscription and subscription services revenue recognized for the second quarter increased 3% to $50.9 million,” while “the total value of contracts signed in the second quarter grew 8% to $53.7 million.”
  • Betjemann highlighted deferred revenue and unbilled deferred revenue as indicators of future recognition: “deferred revenue balance” was “$101.5 million,” and “unbilled deferred revenue contracted for the second quarter… increased 9% to $10.6 million,” with “the total balance… $64.9 million.”
  • Profitability and costs were framed around operating leverage and prior actions. Betjemann reported gross margin of “75.9%,” SG&A of “$41.2 million” (down from “$43.7 million”), and “Adjusted EBITDA… $4.1 million.”
  • Betjemann said the company “incurred $1.5 million in expense for this restructuring activity” and reported “a net loss of $2 million.”
  • Cash flow was a major quarter highlight: Betjemann reported operating cash flow for the first two quarters “increased 28% to $16.4 million” and “Free cash flow for the second quarter was $13.2 million.”

Q&A

  • Alexander Paris, Barrington Research: asked whether management still expects North America/Enterprise invoiced growth to continue through the year; CEO Paul Walker answered, “Yes. We did. Yes.”
  • Paris, Barrington Research: asked about the macro environment vs. last quarter; CEO Walker responded, “it's largely unchanged from what we saw a quarter ago,” and described it as “neutral… better than it was a year ago at this time.”
  • Paris, Barrington Research: pressed on when longer-term guidance could be updated and whether 20% adjusted EBITDA margin remains a target; CFO Jessica Betjemann said, “in the fall and our Q4 call is when we're going to provide the guidance for our fiscal year 2027,” and added, “our goal is to have around 1 point improvement a year,” while CEO Walker said, “we do believe that, that 20%… is still a good number out there.”
  • Jeff Martin, ROTH: asked what is driving Education strength; President Michael Covey cited “3 state-level opportunities,” “large district opportunities,” and said funding partners are “in the range of $20 million a year,” while noting uncertainty around “the Department of Education” and that “ESSER funds… are gone.”
  • Nehal Chokshi, Northland: asked for drivers of free cash flow strength; CFO Betjemann answered, “a very strong positive swing in the net working capital… with regards to the collections on AR.”
  • David Storms, Stonegate: asked if new logos are pilots and about AI-related use cases; CFO Betjemann said, “Very few of our new logos are pilots,” adding, “There's a ton of interest” in AI transformation and “it touches everyone.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ sentiment was slightly positive, with multiple questions reinforcing confidence in invoiced growth and cash generation; Nehal Chokshi called free cash flow “really strong” and highlighted buybacks as “a bold move,” while Alexander Paris framed “2 consecutive quarters of growth in invoiced amounts.”
  • Management sentiment was positive in prepared remarks and steady in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing execution and stability; CEO Paul Walker said, “We feel great about our momentum,” and characterized the macro as “neutral,” while CFO Jessica Betjemann reiterated guidance and seasonality expectations.
  • Compared with the previous quarter, management maintained the “year of execution” framing while speaking with more emphasis on conversion to fiscal 2027 results; CEO Walker again pointed to acceleration next year, and Q&A included more discussion of margin trajectory (“around 1 point improvement a year”) than in Q1.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management kept fiscal 2026 guidance ranges unchanged quarter to quarter, with CFO Jessica Betjemann again affirming “$265 million to $275 million” revenue and “$28 million to $33 million” adjusted EBITDA.
  • Enterprise North America continued to show two consecutive quarters of invoiced growth; in Q2, CEO Paul Walker cited “7% growth in Enterprise North America, or 10% when excluding our government business,” following Q1’s disclosure of “7%” growth and “13%” excluding government.
  • Q2 placed heavier emphasis on cash flow and working-capital swing (including “Free cash flow… $13.2 million”), while Q1 featured a negative free cash flow quarter and heavier restructuring expense; the Q2 discussion also expanded on multiyear contract mix reaching “62%.”
  • Analyst questioning shifted toward longer-term targets and timing of sharing multi-year plans; in Q2, Barrington asked when management would “update” longer-term guidance and whether “20% adjusted EBITDA margin” is still a reasonable target, whereas Q1 focused more on the initial inflection in North America invoicing and sales-force transformation impacts.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited government-related disruption in Enterprise North America: CEO Paul Walker said government business was “impacted by the disruption caused by a reduction in federal spending.”
  • CFO Jessica Betjemann stressed “a disciplined view of the risks and opportunities ahead… in an uncertain macro environment,” tying mitigation to “cost reduction efforts including additional restructuring actions taken this quarter.”
  • Education leadership flagged policy and funding uncertainties: President Michael Covey referenced “some uncertainty with what the Trump administration is going to do,” and said “ESSER funds… are gone,” alongside “declining enrollment in the public sector.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as a continuation of fiscal 2026’s “year of execution,” pointing to 5% consolidated invoiced growth and increased multiyear contracting as building blocks for fiscal 2027 acceleration. Leadership reaffirmed full-year revenue guidance of $265 million to $275 million and adjusted EBITDA of $28 million to $33 million, while emphasizing a back-half weighted earnings profile and outlining cost actions, restructuring, and working-capital dynamics that drove $13.2 million of quarterly free cash flow.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Penguin Solutions raises FY 2026 outlook to 12% net sales growth and $2.15 non-GAAP EPS as memory demand strengthens
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Penguin Solutions (PENG) Q2 fiscal 2026

Management View

  • CEO Kash Shaikh said this was his first earnings call as CEO and that he had “spent significant time with customers, partners and our teams around the world,” adding: “AI is moving from experimentation to production with workloads increasingly shifting towards real-time inference.”
  • Shaikh said the company is “building Penguin into an AI factory platform company,” outlining six elements: ClusterWare, “our new Penguin MemoryAI line,” Advanced Computing Systems, OriginAI factory architectures, “end-to-end services,” and “our partner ecosystem.” He added: “Time to deployment is now directly tied to time to first token.”
  • Shaikh highlighted new product announcements from NVIDIA GTC: “the Penguin MemoryAI server” and “the immediate availability of our new MemoryAI KV Cache server,” plus “the expansion of our OriginAI Factory Architecture portfolio.”
  • Shaikh said the company “recently appointed Ian Colle as Senior Vice President and Chief Product Officer,” noting his background “most recently at Amazon Web Services.”
  • Shaikh summarized Q2: “we delivered net sales of $343 million. Non-GAAP gross margin was 31.2%. Non-GAAP diluted earnings per share were $0.52,” and said the company was “raising the midpoint of our full year net sales and EPS outlook.”
  • CFO Nate Olmstead said: “Non-GAAP gross margin came in at 31.2%,” and added, “We currently project lower gross margins in the second half, driven by a higher mix of lower-margin AI hardware and memory sales, rising memory costs in our AI factory solutions and less tariff cost recovery in LED.”

Outlook

  • Management raised full-year targets, with CFO Nate Olmstead stating the midpoint “now calls for 12% net sales growth and $2.15 of non-GAAP diluted EPS, up from our previous outlook of 6% net sales growth and $2 of non-GAAP diluted EPS.”
  • Segment ranges shifted notably versus last quarter’s framing, with Olmstead saying: “For Advanced Computing, we now expect full year net sales to change between minus 25% and minus 15% year-over-year,” while “for memory, we now expect net sales to grow between 65% and 75% year-over-year,” and LED to “decline between minus 15% and minus 5% year-over-year.”
  • Olmstead updated profitability expectations: “Our non-GAAP gross margin outlook for the full year is now 28%, plus or minus 0.5 percentage points,” and said operating expenses remain “$250 million, plus or minus $5 million,” with “a modest sequential increase” in the second half tied to “increased investments in R&D, including for our ClusterWare software and MemoryAI solutions.”
  • Compared with Q1, the company moved from confirming prior guidance to raising full-company net sales growth and EPS, while also lowering the Advanced Computing full-year range and lifting the memory growth range.

Financial Results

  • Q2 non-GAAP EPS was $0.52 vs. $0.42 (analysts estimate), and net sales were $343 million vs. $339.0 million (analysts estimate).
  • CFO Nate Olmstead reported: “total Penguin Solutions net sales were $343 million, down 6% year-over-year,” with “Non-GAAP operating margin ... 13.2%,” and “Adjusted EBITDA ... $50 million.”
  • By segment, Olmstead said Advanced Computing net sales were $116 million “down 42% year-over-year,” Integrated Memory was $172 million “up strongly with 63% growth year-over-year,” and Optimized LED was $56 million “down 7%.”
  • Olmstead said working-capital levels reflected the memory ramp: “Inventory totaled $322 million ... reflecting increased memory costs, growth in our memory business and strategic purchases,” and noted Q2 operating cash flow of $55 million.
  • On capital allocation and balance sheet, Olmstead said the company received “approximately $32 million ... from proceeds from the disposition of our investment in Celestial AI,” repurchased “approximately 1.7 million shares” for $32 million, ended with “$450 million of debt,” and “closed the quarter in a net cash position,” adding it has “no further scheduled debt payments due until 2029.”

Q&A

  • Katherine Campagna, Goldman Sachs: asked how much of higher memory outlook was pricing vs. demand and how to think about investment impact; Senior VP & CFO Olmstead replied: “for the increase ... that's majority pricing but demand is also very strong,” adding, “to get to the high end of that outlook really just refers to our ability to secure materials.”
  • Brian Chin, Stifel: asked what drove lower Advanced Computing guidance and how booked the company is; CEO Shaikh said “the lag between our bookings and the revenue” and that revenue “lags about 3 to 6 months,” adding: “The main issue at this point is timing.”
  • Matthew Calitri, Needham: asked if new memory launches mark a strategy shift and what supports “durable demand”; CEO Shaikh said MemoryAI is “a part of our strategy” because inference “is memory bound and latency sensitive,” and cited a “Tier 1 financial institution” that bought the “CXL-based KV cache server.”
  • Manmohanpreet Singh, JPMorgan: pressed on whether bookings changed and on NVIDIA reference designs; CFO Olmstead said bookings were “strong in Q2” but “the deployment cycle has lengthened a little bit,” while CEO Shaikh said NVIDIA blueprints are “more complementary to our AI factory platform.”
  • Ananda Baruah, Loop Capital: asked what customers are saying about inference/agentic and whether CXL revenue could arrive sooner; CEO Shaikh said “we believe we are early in the adoption of inference,” and tied accelerating CXL relevance to inference, adding that CXL “allows new memory pooling,” while photonic memory “takes it to the next level.”
  • Kevin Cassidy, Rosenblatt: asked about memory margin dynamics and whether CXL systems should be higher margin; CFO Olmstead cited “stronger demand in flash” and pricing timing, and added on CXL: “Yes, we do. It's really a solution. It's got software aspects to it.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly skeptical, with repeated probing on what changed in Advanced Computing guidance and whether demand vs. pricing and supply constraints were driving memory upside (e.g., Goldman asking “How much of this is from increased favorable pricing versus demand,” and Stifel asking “what changed” and how “booked” the company is).
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more explanatory in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing timing and supply constraints rather than demand weakness, including CEO Shaikh saying “The main issue at this point is timing,” and CFO Olmstead saying the “only inhibitor” to higher memory outlook was “our ability to secure materials.”
  • Versus the prior quarter, management messaging shifted from confirming the full-year outlook to explicitly “raising our full company net sales and non-GAAP diluted EPS outlook,” while analysts kept focus on supply constraints and the conversion of pipeline/bookings into revenue.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Leadership and strategy presentation changed, with Q2 introducing CEO Kash Shaikh and an “AI factory platform” framing, while Q1 emphasized enterprise adoption, diversification, and ICE ClusterWare customization.
  • Guidance moved from Q1 “confirm” to Q2 “raising” full-year net sales growth and non-GAAP EPS, while Q2 also reduced the Advanced Computing segment’s full-year range to a negative growth band and increased the memory growth range.
  • Analysts’ questions were consistent across quarters on supply constraints and timing, but Q2 added more pointed questions on NVIDIA’s reference designs and the immediacy of CXL/inference monetization.

Risks and Concerns

  • CFO Nate Olmstead cited supply and cost pressures: “ongoing supply chain constraints,” “extended lead times,” and “industry-wide higher costs for memory,” which “may slow customer demand ... and may lower our gross margins.”
  • Management flagged second-half margin risk from mix and cost: “higher mix of lower-margin AI hardware and memory sales,” “rising memory costs in our AI factory solutions,” and “less tariff cost recovery in LED.”
  • CEO Shaikh and CFO Olmstead both tied Advanced Computing variability to deployment timing and a longer cycle, with Olmstead noting “the deployment cycle has lengthened a little bit with some of the supply constraints, in particular, on memory.”

Final Takeaway

Management described Q2 as driven by strong Integrated Memory demand and favorable pricing dynamics, alongside a deliberate shift in AI/HPC toward a diversified enterprise, neocloud, and sovereign customer mix. The company raised its full-year outlook to 12% net sales growth and $2.15 in non-GAAP EPS, while describing Advanced Computing revenue timing and supply constraints as near-term gating items. The CEO positioned new MemoryAI and ClusterWare investments as central to an AI inference-led “AI factory platform” strategy, with management repeatedly emphasizing that material availability and deployment cadence will influence second-half results and margins.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil prices slide ahead of Trump TV address that may see war curtailed in 2-3 weeks
data.description:
Crude oil futures closed lower in a volatile session Wednesday ahead of President Trump’s televised address on the Iran war at 9 p.m. ET.

Trump has signaled he wants to end the conflict in 2-3 weeks, even as the vital Strait of Hormuz chokepoint remains largely closed to shipping.

Trump posted earlier Wednesday that Iran's president has asked the U.S. for a ceasefire, which he would consider only after the strait is no longer blocked, and Iranian state media said the foreign ministry called the statement false.

Market participants are betting the president would not allow ⁠oil supply disruptions caused by the war to extend into mid-May, when U.S. gasoline demand is typically at its strongest, SEB ​analysts said in a note.

"The risk to U.S. gasoline prices, consumer sentiment, and ultimately the November midterm elections makes a prolonged conflict politically costly," SEB said.

This week, U.S. ​gasoline prices rose above $4/gal on average for the first time in more than three years.

Analysts believe energy flows through the Strait ‌of Hormuz ⁠would be slow to return to pre-war levels even if a ceasefire is announced.

"Odds appear to lean in the direction of a U.S. Iranian war exit but... the status of the Strait of Hormuz remains highly uncertain and much deserving of some geo-risk premium even if global oil supplies slowly begin to loosen," Ritterbusch and Associates said in a note.

OPEC said crude oil production of its members plunged by 7.5M bbl/day in March from the ​previous month, as producers ⁠were forced to cut output because storage is full.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery closed down 1.2% to $100.12/bbl, and has dipped below $99 after hours, while front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery finished down 2.7% to $101.16/bbl.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) fell for a third straight session, with the front-month Nymex May contract closing down 2.2% to $2.819/MMBtu, its lowest settlement value since September 22.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Skillz outlines $130M debt maturity review while highlighting RZR’s first full-year positive adjusted EBITDA
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Skillz (SKLZ) Q4 2025

Management View

  • “For the fourth quarter of 2025, GAAP revenue was $30 million, up 11% from $27 million in the third quarter and up 67% from $18 million in the prior year period.” (Co-Founder, CEO & Chairman of the Board Andrew Paradise)
  • “A key driver of 2025 was our AI ad tech segment, RZR, which was rebranded from Aarki last month. RZR delivered 146% net revenue growth year-over-year. And for the first time since its 2021 acquisition, it generated positive adjusted EBITDA for the full year 2025.” (CEO Paradise)
  • “Last month, at the Annual Game Developers Conference in San Francisco, we debuted our Pro SDK product.” (CEO Paradise)
  • “We recently strengthened our Board of Directors with the addition of Gary Vecchiarelli and Shannon Demus.” (CEO Paradise)
  • “Regarding Papaya, our trial is now set for April 13, 2026, in the Southern District of New York, and we look very much forward to our day in court.” (CEO Paradise)
  • “As a reminder, in connection with our 2024 settlement with AviaGame, our annual $7.5 million payment was received in Q1 of 2026. To date, a total of $65 million has been received from AviaGame.” (CEO Paradise)
  • “We ended Q4 2025 with $195 million in cash and cash equivalents and $130 million of debt outstanding that is now classified as current.” (Chief Financial Officer Gaetano Franceschi)

Outlook

  • The transcript did not include formal revenue or EPS guidance for 2026; management framed the near-term focus as capital structure actions and continued efficiency gains. “As the debt approaches maturity later this year, we continue to evaluate a range of strategic alternatives to optimize our capital structure.” (CFO Franceschi)
  • Management’s profitability framing leaned on RZR’s shift to positive full-year adjusted EBITDA and continued platform improvements. “With RZR achieving positive full year adjusted EBITDA paired with continued improvements across the Skillz platform, we're making progress on our path to profitability.” (CEO Paradise)
  • From the prior quarter, the most explicit outlook change was the emergence of a 2026 legal milestone and cash collection timing: the Papaya trial date (April 13, 2026) and the confirmation that the AviaGame $7.5 million annual payment was received in Q1 2026, with “two additional payments of $7.5 million in each of March 2027 and March 2028.” (CEO Paradise)

Financial Results

  • Q4 2025 GAAP revenue was $30 million; adjusted EBITDA loss was $10 million; net loss was $18 million. (CEO Paradise; CFO Franceschi)
  • Expense lines highlighted in Q4 included research and development expense of $6 million, sales and marketing expense of $19 million, and general and administrative expense of $18 million. (CFO Franceschi)
  • Skillz’s paying monthly active users (PMAU) were 141,000 in Q4 2025, with management describing both a platform-content disruption and a technical issue affecting engagement tools. “Paying monthly active users or PMAU, was 141,000, down 9% from 155,000 in the third quarter and up from 110,000 in the prior year period.” (CEO Paradise)
  • Liquidity and debt classification were emphasized as a key financial topic. “We ended Q4 2025 with $195 million in cash and cash equivalents and $130 million of debt outstanding that is now classified as current.” (CFO Franceschi)

Q&A

  • Edward Alter, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: Would love to dig into the Skillz side, with paying MAUs and GMV trends flipping in Q4; what changed and is this the trajectory? Unknown Executive: “in Q4, we had one of our larger gaming developers leave the platform. So we had a little bit of a dip in our paying MAU. But you can see that we continue to increase on our GMV per paying MAU.”
  • Edward Alter, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: On the partner leaving the platform (disclosed as 51% of last year’s revenue), what is the progress moving players into Skillz-branded versions and how has the rollout gone? Unknown Executive: “We don't disclose like the transition for a variety of reasons. But basically, when the partner left the platform, there were some games that left immediately. And then the two larger games that are, call it, the majority, call it, 80-plus percent are there, and we're in the process of transitioning to our owned games.”
  • Edward Alter, Jefferies LLC, Research Division: Follow-up on PMAU drivers in Q4? CEO Paradise: “we had a technical issue with some of our engagement and marketing technologies for our player base, and we've now addressed that.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Slightly negative analyst tone, centered on concentration and transition risk after a major developer exit and the mechanics behind PMAU and GMV shifts. “one of our larger gaming developers leave the platform” was the core issue management cited in response. (Unknown Executive)
  • Neutral-to-slightly positive management tone in prepared remarks, emphasizing sequential revenue growth, RZR momentum, and progress toward profitability, while using direct explanations in Q&A when pressed. “we're making progress on our path to profitability.” (CEO Paradise)
  • Compared with the prior quarter, the current call’s tone was more operationally specific on content disruption (developer departure) and tooling issues, while the prior quarter carried heavier process and compliance framing around delayed filings and NYSE notice. “we received a notice from the NYSE that the company was not in compliance with its listing standards.” (Joseph Jaffoni, JCIR)

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The company moved from describing Aarki to stating, “RZR, which was rebranded from Aarki last month,” and tied the segment to “146% net revenue growth year-over-year” and “positive adjusted EBITDA for the full year 2025.” (CEO Paradise)
  • Skillz platform dynamics shifted: Q3 PMAU was 155,000 and described as growing sequentially, while Q4 PMAU was 141,000, with management attributing the decline to “one of our larger gaming developers” leaving and “a technical issue” in engagement and marketing technologies that was “addressed.” (CEO Paradise; Unknown Executive)
  • Analyst engagement increased in Q4 (one Jefferies question set) versus no analyst questions included in the Q3 transcript Q&A section.
  • Capital structure was more prominent in Q4 with debt “classified as current” and management “evaluate a range of strategic alternatives,” versus Q3’s balance sheet snapshot without the same maturity emphasis. (CFO Franceschi)

Risks and Concerns

  • Customer/developer concentration and transition execution risk was highlighted in Q&A, with management confirming “one of our larger gaming developers leave the platform” and describing ongoing moves “transitioning to our owned games.” (Unknown Executive)
  • Operational risk surfaced via the disclosed tooling disruption: “we had a technical issue with some of our engagement and marketing technologies for our player base.” (CEO Paradise)
  • Legal risk and timing remained a theme, with management stating the Papaya trial is “set for April 13, 2026,” and reaffirming ongoing litigation against Papaya and Voodoo. (CEO Paradise)
  • Refinancing or repayment risk was implied by debt classification and maturity timing. “As the debt approaches maturity later this year, we continue to evaluate a range of strategic alternatives to optimize our capital structure.” (CFO Franceschi)

Final Takeaway

Management highlighted Q4 sequential revenue growth and reduced adjusted EBITDA loss, positioned RZR (rebranded from Aarki) as a principal growth and profitability driver with full-year positive adjusted EBITDA, and emphasized near-term focus on capital structure decisions as $130 million of debt is classified as current and approaches maturity later in 2026. In Q&A, management attributed Q4 PMAU softness to a large developer leaving the platform and a technical issue in engagement and marketing technologies, while describing an ongoing transition toward owned games and maintaining focus on efficiency and disciplined scaling.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Artemis II lifts off, carrying astronauts on historic return to the Moon
data.description:
Astronauts blasted off on a long-awaited journey toward the moon in a mission NASA hopes will mark the beginning of a new chapter in human space exploration after years of delays.

The agency’s Space Launch System rocket lifted off at roughly 6:30 p.m. ET Wednesday, sending a towering column of flame and smoke into the sky as its engines and boosters powered the vehicle upward.

Soon after launch, the rocket’s upper stage detached from the core and continued onward with the Orion crew capsule attached. That stage then began a series of maneuvers in orbit, preparing the spacecraft for a trajectory that will carry the crew more than 250,000 miles (402,336 km) from Earth.

The Artemis II mission is designed to send astronauts around the moon and safely back home, representing the farthest distance humans have traveled in space in over 50 years.

As part of the flight plan, Orion will first circle Earth before heading toward lunar orbit. At its closest approach, the spacecraft is expected to come within about 5,000 miles (8,046.72 km) of the moon’s surface.

The mission is a key step in NASA’s broader effort to return astronauts to the moon. The Artemis program aims to establish a sustained human presence there and eventually support deeper space exploration missions.

On board Orion are NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover and Christina Koch, along with Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen. The crew has spent years preparing for the mission, which will make them the first humans to travel to the vicinity of the moon since the Apollo era.

Although Artemis II will not attempt a lunar landing, it is intended to pave the way for a future mission that will put astronauts on the moon’s surface, currently targeted for later this decade.

Unlike the Apollo program, which unfolded during Cold War competition with the Soviet Union, NASA’s renewed lunar effort is taking place amid rising competition with China, which is also pursuing plans to establish a research presence on the moon.
